## 🔒 Trivy Security Scan Results — `docker-sonic-mgmt:latest`

**Scan date**: 2026-04-10 | **Image**: `docker-sonic-mgmt:latest` (Ubuntu 24.04) | **Docker CE**: 29.4.0
**Scan command**: `trivy image docker-sonic-mgmt:latest --scanners vuln --ignore-unfixed`

### What this PR fixes

| CVE | Severity | Package | Before | After | Fix |
|-----|----------|---------|--------|-------|-----|
| CVE-2025-4565 | **HIGH** | protobuf | 4.24.4 | 7.34.1 | Force-upgrade after batch pip install |
| CVE-2026-0994 | **HIGH** | protobuf | 4.24.4 | 7.34.1 | Same |
| CVE-2026-39892 | MEDIUM | cryptography | 46.0.6 | 46.0.7 | Patch Azure CLI bundled `/opt/az` + remove stale metadata |
| CVE-2025-8869 | MEDIUM | pip | 24.0 | 26.0.1 | `pip install --upgrade pip` |
| OS packages | various | libssl, etc. | unpatched | patched | `apt-get upgrade -y` |

### Changes made
1. **`apt-get upgrade -y`** — upgrades all vulnerable OS packages (same pattern as docker-ptf)
2. **`pip install --upgrade pip`** — fixes CVE-2025-8869
3. **`cryptography>=46.0.7`** — pinned in pip install + patched Azure CLI's bundled copy
4. **`protobuf>=6.33.5`** — pinned in pip install + force-upgraded separately (batch install constrains to 4.x due to opentelemetry deps)

### Remaining vulnerabilities (unfixable)

#### ansible 11.10.0 → 12.2.0 (MEDIUM, SKIP)
- Ansible 12.x is a **major version upgrade** (ansible-core 2.18 → 2.19)
- The Dockerfile patches `ansible/plugins/loader.py` — this patch will not apply cleanly on ansible 12.x
- sonic-mgmt playbooks and roles need separate validation for ansible-core 2.19 compatibility
- **Decision**: Skip — requires dedicated validation effort, not safe for a security-only PR

#### Docker binaries — docker-buildx, docker-compose (4 HIGH, upstream)

| CVE | Severity | Package | Installed | Fix | Binary |
|-----|----------|---------|-----------|-----|--------|
| CVE-2026-34040 | HIGH | docker/docker | v28.5.2 | 29.3.1 | buildx + compose |
| CVE-2026-39883 | HIGH | otel/sdk | v1.40.0–v1.42.0 | 1.43.0 | buildx + compose |
| + 6 MEDIUM | MEDIUM | stdlib | v1.25.8 | 1.25.9+ | docker + buildx + compose |

- **docker-ce-cli 29.4.0** is already the latest from Docker's apt repo
- **docker-buildx-plugin 0.33.0** and **docker-compose-plugin 5.1.2** are the latest available
- These are pre-built Go binaries shipped by Docker Inc — vulns are baked in upstream
- **Decision**: Upstream issue — Docker Inc needs to rebuild with Go 1.26.2+ and otel 1.43.0+

### Summary

| | Before | After | Delta |
|--|--------|-------|-------|
| Python HIGH | 2 | 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025 2026 ✅ | -2 |
| Python MEDIUM | 3 | 2016 2017 2018 2019 2021 | -2 |
| Go (upstream) | 29 | 29 | unfixable |
| **Total** | **35** | **30** | **-5 fixed** |

## 🔒 Trivy Security Scan Results — `docker-ptf:latest`

**Scan date**: 2026-04-09 | **Image**: `docker-ptf:latest` (Debian 12.13) | **Go**: 1.25.9
**Scan command**: `trivy image docker-ptf:latest --scanners vuln --ignore-unfixed`

### Summary

| Target | Type | Total | Critical | High | Medium | Low | Unknown |
|--------|------|-------|----------|------|--------|-----|---------|
| debian 12.13 (OS) | debian | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| All Python packages | python-pkg | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| usr/local/bin/gnoic | gobinary | 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025 2026 ✅ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| usr/local/bin/grpcurl | gobinary | 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025 2026 ✅ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| usr/local/bin/gnmic | gobinary | 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025 2026 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| usr/local/bin/influxd | gobinary | 2024 | 2 | 6 | 9 | 1 | 6 |

---

### ✅ Clean Binaries
- **gnoic** — 0 vulnerabilities
- **grpcurl** — 0 vulnerabilities

---

### gnmic — 2 remaining (unfixable)

| CVE | Severity | Package | Installed | Fix | Status |
|-----|----------|---------|-----------|-----|--------|
| CVE-2026-34040 | HIGH | github.com/docker/docker | v28.5.2+incompatible | 29.3.1 | **No fix** — v29 not published as Go module tag |
| CVE-2026-33997 | MEDIUM | github.com/docker/docker | v28.5.2+incompatible | 29.3.1 | **No fix** — same reason |

> `github.com/docker/docker` uses `+incompatible` versioning. `@latest` resolves to v28.5.2. Docker Engine v29 tags are not available as Go module versions. No backport exists for v28.x.

---

### influxd — 24 remaining (all upstream, pre-built binary)

**InfluxDB v2.8.0 is the latest v2.x release** (Dec 2025). Next is v3.x which is architecturally incompatible. The binary is downloaded pre-built from `dl.influxdata.com` — we cannot patch its dependencies.

| CVE | Severity | Package | Installed | Fix |
|-----|----------|---------|-----------|-----|
| CVE-2026-33186 | **CRITICAL** | google.golang.org/grpc | v1.64.1 | 1.79.3 |
| CVE-2025-68121 | **CRITICAL** | stdlib (Go 1.24.9) | v1.24.9 | 1.24.13+ |
| CVE-2025-61726 | HIGH | stdlib | v1.24.9 | 1.24.12+ |
| CVE-2025-61728 | HIGH | stdlib | v1.24.9 | 1.24.12+ |
| CVE-2025-61729 | HIGH | stdlib | v1.24.9 | 1.24.11+ |
| CVE-2026-25679 | HIGH | stdlib | v1.24.9 | 1.25.8+ |
| CVE-2026-32285 | HIGH | github.com/buger/jsonparser | v1.1.1 | 1.1.2 |
| CVE-2025-22868 | HIGH | golang.org/x/oauth2 | v0.21.0 | 0.27.0 |
| GHSA-xmrv-pmrh-hhx2 | MEDIUM | aws-sdk-go-v2 eventstream | v1.6.2 | 1.7.8 |
| GHSA-xmrv-pmrh-hhx2 | MEDIUM | aws-sdk-go-v2 service/s3 | v1.54.4 | 1.97.3 |
| CVE-2025-10543 | MEDIUM | paho.mqtt.golang | v1.2.0 | 1.5.1 |
| CVE-2024-6104 | MEDIUM | go-retryablehttp | v0.6.4 | 0.7.7 |
| CVE-2025-47914 | MEDIUM | golang.org/x/crypto | v0.39.0 | 0.45.0 |
| CVE-2025-58181 | MEDIUM | golang.org/x/crypto | v0.39.0 | 0.45.0 |
| + 10 more LOW/UNKNOWN | | stdlib / misc | | |

> **Risk**: influxd is used for HFT E2E tests in the PTF container only, not exposed externally. Track upstream InfluxDB v2 releases for a potential v2.8.1+ patch.

**Merge order:** This PR should merge **before** the companion sonic-utilities PR (sonic-net/sonic-utilities#4436). The utilities PR removes the forced `IJSON_BACKEND=python` override — without the Debian `python3-ijson` package installed by this PR, ijson would auto-detect `yajl2_cffi` which crashes under monit's `MemoryDenyWriteExecute=true`.

**Merge order:** This PR should merge **before** the companion sonic-utilities PR (sonic-net/sonic-utilities#4436). This PR makes the fast `yajl2_c` backend available. The utilities PR then removes the forced `python` backend override to use it.

If the utilities PR merges first, images built before this PR merges would lose the forced python backend fallback while pip's ijson (without the C extension) is still present — causing ijson to auto-detect `yajl2_cffi`, which crashes under monit's `MemoryDenyWriteExecute=true`.

On 4/9/26 8:57 PM, Qi Luo wrote:
>
> ***@***.**** commented on this pull request.
>
> ------------------------------------------------------------------------
>
> In src/sonic-yang-mgmt/sonic_yang_ext.py 
> <https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/pull/26563#discussion_r3061456399>:
>
> > @@ -132,10 +132,14 @@ def _preProcessYangGrouping(self, moduleName, module):
> 
> for grouping in groupings:
> gName = ***@***.***"]
> - self.preProcessedYang['grouping'][moduleName][gName] = dict()
> - self.preProcessedYang['grouping'][moduleName][gName]["leaf"] = grouping.get('leaf')
> - self.preProcessedYang['grouping'][moduleName][gName]["leaf-list"] = grouping.get('leaf-list')
> - self.preProcessedYang['grouping'][moduleName][gName]["choice"] = grouping.get('choice')
> + gdata = dict()
> + # Extract all possible child node types from the grouping
> + for node_type in ['leaf', 'leaf-list', 'choice', 'container',
> + 'list', 'uses']:
>
> (I was trying to expidite the code review so you can merge critical 
> code change sooner. If the change is purely a refactoring, I can 
> easily approve this PR. If you want to mix into this PR, its okay.)
>
> Nested |uses| resolution only works when the parent already has 
> *multiple* |uses| clauses.
>
> When a grouping contains a nested |uses|, 
> |_compileUsesClauseList(model, groupdata, 'uses', ...)| appends it to 
> |model['uses']| /during/ iteration of |uses_s|. This works in 
> |test-grouping.yang| because |TEST_GROUPING_LIST| has 3 |uses| → 
> xmltodict produces a *list*, |uses_s| aliases that same list object, 
> and the appended item gets picked up by the |for uses in uses_s:| loop.
>
> But if a node has a *single* |uses nested-uses-group;|:
>
> 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025 2026 parse_reviews.py pr_dump.sh repo_list.sh repos update.sh xmltodict produces a dict for |model["uses"]|
> 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025 2026 parse_reviews.py pr_dump.sh repo_list.sh repos update.sh |uses_s = [uses_s]| creates a /new/ local list
> 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025 2026 parse_reviews.py pr_dump.sh repo_list.sh repos update.sh |_compileUsesClauseList| does |model['uses'] = [model['uses']]|
> (another new list) and appends the nested |uses| there
> 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025 2026 parse_reviews.py pr_dump.sh repo_list.sh repos update.sh the |for uses in uses_s:| loop never sees it
> 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025 2026 parse_reviews.py pr_dump.sh repo_list.sh repos update.sh |del model["uses"]| then drops it unresolved
>
> *Suggested fix* — normalize |model["uses"]| to a list /before/ 
> capturing |uses_s| so they alias the same object:
>
> uses_s = model.get("uses")
> if not uses_s:
> return
> if isinstance(uses_s,dict):
> model["uses"]= [uses_s]
> uses_s = model["uses"]
>
> (or alternatively re-loop while |model.get("uses")| is non-empty)
>
> Also worth adding a test case in |test-grouping.yang| with a container 
> that has a /single/ |uses| referencing a grouping that itself contains 
> |uses|, to cover this path.

## Code Review

### High

1. **`cli/clear/plugins/clear_dhcp_relay.py` — `SUPPORTED_DHCP_TYPE`**: Adding "Malformed" and "Ignored" is a **silent behavioral change for v4 clear**. Previously `clear dhcp_relay ipv4 counters` did not zero these; now it does. If operators rely on accumulated Malformed/Ignored counts for diagnostics, this clears data they may need. Please document this as a breaking change and confirm it's intentional.

2. **`cli/show/plugins/show_dhcp_relay.py` — `dhcp_relay_ip6counters`**: **Breaking change**: `show dhcp_relay ipv6 counters` now reads dhcpmon COUNTERS_DB instead of dhcp6relay STATE_DB. If dhcpmon v6 isn't deployed, this command returns nothing where it previously showed dhcp6relay counters. The old behavior is only accessible via `show dhcp6relay_counters counts`. This should be explicitly documented as a migration in the changelog and release notes.

### Medium

3. **`cli/clear/plugins/clear_dhcp_relay.py` — `notify_dhcpmon_processes`**: Sends SIGUSR1/SIGUSR2 to **all** dhcpmon processes regardless of v4/v6. A v4-only dhcpmon instance will unnecessarily pause and re-sync when clearing v6 counters. Consider filtering by the process command line (`-4`/`-6` flag) to scope the signal.

4. **`cli/show/plugins/show_dhcp_relay.py`**: Display types intentionally exclude "Malformed"/"Ignored" but the `clear` command now zeros them. Users can clear fields they can never see via `show`. Consider adding `--verbose` or `--all` flag to `show`, or at minimum document this asymmetry.

5. **`cli-plugin-tests/test_clear_dhcp_relay.py` — `test_clear_dhcpmon_db_counters`**: Only tested with v4 constants (`DHCPV4_COUNTER_TABLE_PREFIX`, `SUPPORTED_DHCP_TYPE`). Since this is now a shared function, it should also be parameterized to test with v6 constants (`DHCPV6_COUNTER_TABLE_PREFIX`, `SUPPORTED_DHCPV6_TYPE`).

6. **`cli-plugin-tests/test_clear_dhcp_relay.py` — `test_clear_dhcp_relay_ipv6_counters`**: The assertion was weakened — only checks the 2nd positional arg. Does NOT verify v6-specific constants (lock file, state table) are passed correctly. A regression where v4 constants are accidentally used for v6 would go undetected.

### Low

7. **`cli/show/plugins/show_dhcp_relay.py` — `get_dhcpmon_vlans`**: `db.keys()` can return `None` in some SonicV2Connector implementations. `for key in None` → `TypeError`. Adding `or []` guard is a one-line fix. Same applies to `clear_dhcpmon_db_counters`.

8. **`cli/clear/plugins/clear_dhcp_relay.py` — `clear_dhcpmon_counters` finally block**: `fcntl.flock(lock_file, LOCK_UN)` is called unconditionally even if locking failed. On Linux this is a no-op but semantically incorrect. Gate on `locked`.

---

Overall the refactoring from duplicated v4/v6 functions into shared parameterized versions is clean. The CLI wiring is correct. **Items 1–2 are the most important** — the behavioral changes on existing commands should be clearly documented to avoid surprising operators during upgrade.

@deepak-singhal0408 , is there an issue created on FRR and this additional delay can be configured/removed in future?

> @deepak-singhal0408 , is there an issue created on FRR and this additional delay can be configured/removed in future?

yes, frr issue link: https://github.com/FRRouting/frr/issues/21298
Yes, wanted to give this setting(50ms) enough soak time in prod, and if all goes well, we could set it to 0 or have a configurable knob(could decide on this before 202611)..
how this change will work if someone is using fib suppression feature ?

> how this change will work if someone is using fib suppression feature ?

this change will affect both cases fib-supression off/on in same way.. since the config "bgp suppress-fib-pending" is always present(irrespective of the fact whether fib supression is enabled or not), the current code adds 1second delay before it starts advertisememnt(I assume to pack more NLRIs in single update message), but 1Sec is too long. So after this change, both cases will have 50ms timer running to accommodate additional bulking on top of what we get implicitly. So for fib-supress on case, the advertisement time for first batch would be (50ms+feedback from asic time) vs fib supress off case this time would be (50ms).

@venky-nexthop will be helpful if you could provide little bit more info on the why "Adding buffers_defaults for t0 topo in HWSKU NH-5010" ?

> @venky-nexthop will be helpful if you could provide little bit more info on the why "Adding buffers_defaults for t0 topo in HWSKU NH-5010" ?

Sure, Here is the reason for this PR:

config load_minigraph fails on NH-5010-F-O64 devices when the topology type resolves to t0 (e.g., dut_type: ToRRouter). The Jinja2 template rendering fails because buffers_defaults_t0.j2 is not present in any of the search paths.

Seeing this issue when deploying a minigraph for PTP topology to the device via sonic-mgmt. The PTP topology uses dut_type: ToRRouter, which maps to topology type t0.

@XuChen-MSFT Thank you for the thorough review! Here's how each item was addressed:

**1. Missing `.dep` file** — Fixed in commit [`0b2ffc238`](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/pull/26215/commits/0b2ffc238). Added `platform/broadcom/docker-syncd-brcm-legacy-th-rpc.dep` following the same pattern as `docker-syncd-brcm-rpc.dep`.

**2. `$(SSWSYNCD)` dependency placement** — Fixed in commit [`0b2ffc238`](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/pull/26215/commits/0b2ffc238). Moved `$(SSWSYNCD)` inside the `ifeq ($(INSTALL_DEBUG_TOOLS), y)` block to be consistent with `docker-syncd-brcm-rpc.mk`. The RPC container is used for SAI testing and does not require `sswsyncd` in non-debug builds.

**3. `_AFTER` build ordering** — After further investigation, we found that `_LOAD_DOCKERS` already implies the build ordering dependency (the base image must be built first). The standard `docker-syncd-brcm-rpc.mk` also uses `_LOAD_DOCKERS` without any `_AFTER` directive, confirming it is not needed. Therefore `_AFTER` was not added (removed in commit [`c9fa32809`](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/pull/26215/commits/c9fa32809)).

Addressed all 4 review comments in commit 82ef792:

1. **Sort ports** (`PORT|sort`): ✅ Applied. Ports are now iterated in deterministic sorted order.

2. **Filter special ports**: ✅ Added `startswith` checks for `Ethernet-IB`, `Ethernet-Rec`, `Ethernet-BP` — matching lldpmgrd's `inband_prefix()`, `recirc_prefix()`, `backplane_prefix()` filter logic. Updated comment to document scope.

3. **Fallback for missing alias**: ✅ Uses `PORT[port_name].alias|default('')` with an else branch that falls back to `port_name` — same behavior as lldpmgrd's `generate_pending_lldp_config_cmd_for_port()`.

4. **Unit test**: ✅ Added `mgmt_iface_ipv4_with_ports.json` test input with:
 - Normal ports with aliases (`Ethernet0`, `Ethernet4`)
 - Port with empty alias (`Ethernet8`) → falls back to port name
 - Port with no alias key (`Ethernet12`) → falls back to port name
 - Special ports (`Ethernet-IB0`, `Ethernet-Rec0`, `Ethernet-BP0`) → excluded from output
 
 Expected output in `lldpd-ipv4-iface-with-ports.conf` validates all scenarios.

> ## Review
> **Change 1 — PID removal from logger**: ✅ LGTM. The original `logger -i "$$"` caused the shell PID to appear as the first word of every log message (e.g., `98 static route nexthop not resolved...`), which is confusing. Removing both `-i` and `"$$"` is the right call — the `-t arp_update` tag is sufficient for filtering.
> 
> **Change 2 — Removing the diagnostic log line**: ⚠️ This contradicts @prsunny's feedback to keep the logs. The removed line:
> 
> ```shell
> if [[ -z $(echo ${neigh_state} | grep 'STALE') ]]; then
> logger "static route nexthop not resolved ($neigh_state), pinging $nexthop on $interface"
> fi
> ```
> 
> provides useful diagnostic info — it tells you the neighbor state (INCOMPLETE, REACHABLE, DELAY, etc.) when a static route nexthop needs resolution. The `run_with_retry_cmd` below only logs on retry/failure, so without this line you lose visibility into _why_ pings are being sent.
> 
> **Suggestion**: Keep the log line but without the PID confusion (which is already fixed by Change 1). The log will now cleanly show:
> 
> ```
> arp_update: static route nexthop not resolved (INCOMPLETE), pinging 10.0.0.1 on Ethernet0
> ```
> 
> instead of the old confusing:
> 
> ```
> arp_update: 98 static route nexthop not resolved (INCOMPLETE), pinging 10.0.0.1 on Ethernet0
> ```
> 
> **TL;DR**: Please restore the 3 removed lines (the diagnostic log) per @prsunny's request. The PID fix alone already addresses the original issue.

I think I'm missing something: if, at runtime, `resolv-config.sh` is recreating the symlink, and is running `resolvconf -u`, then why do some containers still not have a properly-populated `/etc/resolv.conf`?

Because `update.d/libc` has two sequential gate checks with a file write between them.
The two gates are satisfied at different times but never in the same invocation — and the intermediate write permanently prevents the second invocation from reaching Gate 2.

The relevant code is in [`/etc/resolvconf/update.d/libc`](https://sources.debian.org/src/resolvconf/1.94/etc/resolvconf/update.d/libc) (resolvconf 1.94, Trixie). The execution order of the three critical sections:

1. **[L142-146](https://sources.debian.org/src/resolvconf/1.94/etc/resolvconf/update.d/libc#L142-146) — Gate 1: content-unchanged check**
 ```sh
 if [ -f "$DYNAMICRSLVCNFFILE" ] && [ "$(cat $TMPFILE)" = "$(cat $DYNAMICRSLVCNFFILE)" ] ; then
 rm -f "$TMPFILE"
 exit 0
 fi
 ```
 Compares newly generated content (`$TMPFILE`) with the existing `/run/resolvconf/resolv.conf`.
 If identical, exits.

2. **[L149](https://sources.debian.org/src/resolvconf/1.94/etc/resolvconf/update.d/libc#L149) — File write (between the two gates)**
 ```sh
 mv -f "$TMPFILE" "$DYNAMICRSLVCNFFILE"
 ```
 Persists the new content to `/run/resolvconf/resolv.conf` **before** the symlink check.
 Once written, all future invocations will see "unchanged" at Gate 1.

3. **[L153](https://sources.debian.org/src/resolvconf/1.94/etc/resolvconf/update.d/libc#L153) — Gate 2: symlink check**
 ```sh
 resolv_conf_is_symlinked_to_dynamic_file || exit 0
 ```
 Where [`resolv_conf_is_symlinked_to_dynamic_file`](https://sources.debian.org/src/resolvconf/1.94/etc/resolvconf/update.d/libc#L60) is:
 ```sh
 [ -L ${ETC}/resolv.conf ] && [ "$(readlink -m ${ETC}/resolv.conf)" = "$DYNAMICRSLVCNFFILE" ]
 ```
 If `/etc/resolv.conf` is not a symlink to `/run/resolvconf/resolv.conf`, exits without
 running `update-libc.d/` (which contains `update-containers`).

The ordering of L149 before L153 is the root of the deadlock: a Gate 2 failure commits the
content to disk, making any subsequent invocation fail at Gate 1 regardless of whether the
symlink has since been created.


### Containers start before DHCP — all read empty `/etc/resolv.conf`

```
21:16:31.356832 resolv-config: [update-containers:main] Updating resolv.conf for container database
21:16:37.943833 resolv-config: [update-containers:main] Updating resolv.conf for container restapi
21:16:39.007647 resolv-config: [update-containers:main] Updating resolv.conf for container swss
......
```

Each of these is `postStartAction` calling `update-containers $DOCKERNAME`. The script does
`RESOLV_CONTENT=$(cat /etc/resolv.conf)` — but `/etc/resolv.conf` is a regular empty file
(from `build_debian.sh`'s `touch`), so every container gets empty DNS written into it.

10 containers running with empty DNS. DHCP hasn't completed yet. The
`libc` → `update-containers` bulk update is supposed to fix them once DHCP finishes.


### DHCP completes at 21:16:53 — bulk update blocked by Gate 2

```
21:16:53.379431 dhclient[9279]: DHCPDISCOVER on eth0 to 255.255.255.255 port 67 interval 8
21:16:53.381622 dhclient[9279]: DHCPOFFER of 10.210.25.14 from 10.222.36.70
21:16:53.381658 dhclient[9279]: DHCPREQUEST for 10.210.25.14 on eth0 to 255.255.255.255 port 67
21:16:53.384519 dhclient[9279]: DHCPACK of 10.210.25.14 from 10.222.36.70
21:16:53.453073 dhclient[9279]: bound to 10.210.25.14 -- renewal in 10641 seconds.
```

dhclient hook calls `resolvconf -a eth0.dhclient` → triggers `update.d/libc`:

| Step | What happens | State |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Generate new content: `nameserver 10.211.0.163`, `10.211.0.164`, `10.222.36.70` | |
| 2 (Gate 1) | Compare with `/run/resolvconf/resolv.conf` (empty at boot) → **different → PASS** | |
| 3 (write) | `mv $TMPFILE /run/resolvconf/resolv.conf` — **file now has DNS content** | `/run/.../resolv.conf` = DNS |
| 4 (Gate 2) | `[ -L /etc/resolv.conf ]` → **FALSE** (regular file from `touch`) → **FAIL → exit** | `/etc/resolv.conf` = empty regular file |
| 5 | never reached — `update-containers` not called | |

Evidence the bulk update did NOT fire: grepping `raw_logs/syslog.log` for `"Starting resolv.conf update for running containers"` returns **zero matches** between 21:16:53 and 21:18:01. Only individual `"Updating resolv.conf for container"` entries exist.

10 running containers still have empty DNS. `/run/resolvconf/resolv.conf` now has the
correct content, but it will never be different again — this is the latch that blocks all
future invocations at Gate 1.


### resolv-config.service runs at 21:18:01 — blocked by Gate 1

```
syslog.log:461 2026-03-10T21:16:26.581866 systemd[1]: Started hostcfgd.timer - Delays hostcfgd daemon until SONiC has started.
syslog.log:41100 2026-03-10T21:17:54.960809 systemd[1]: Started hostcfgd.service - Host config enforcer daemon.
syslog.log:41263 2026-03-10T21:18:01.062910 systemd[1]: Starting resolv-config.service - Update DNS configuration...
syslog.log:41274 2026-03-10T21:18:02.164828 systemd[1]: Finished resolv-config.service - Update DNS configuration.
```

`resolv-config.sh` executes:

```sh
# resolv-config.sh line 10 — creates the symlink
ln -sf /run/resolvconf/resolv.conf /etc/resolv.conf

# resolv-config.sh line 51 — triggers update.d/libc
resolvconf -u
```

`update.d/libc` runs again:

| Step | What happens |
|---|---|
| 1 | Generate new content: same `nameserver 10.211.0.163...` |
| 2 (Gate 1) | Compare with `/run/resolvconf/resolv.conf` — **identical** (written at 21:16:53) → **FAIL → exit** |
| 3 | never reached |
| 4 (Gate 2) | never reached (would PASS now — symlink exists) |
| 5 | never reached — `update-containers` not called |

10 containers still have empty DNS. The symlink now exists, but Gate 1's content-unchanged optimization exits before Gate 2 is ever evaluated.


### Late containers start after 21:18:02 — these get correct DNS

```
syslog.log:41742 2026-03-10T21:18:36.445352 resolv-config: [update-containers:main] Updating resolv.conf for container gnmi
syslog.log:41842 2026-03-10T21:18:39.153862 resolv-config: [update-containers:main] Updating resolv.conf for container lldp
syslog.log:42056 2026-03-10T21:18:41.989390 resolv-config: [update-containers:main] Updating resolv.conf for container mgmt-framework
syslog.log:42550 2026-03-10T21:18:48.188108 resolv-config: [update-containers:main] Updating resolv.conf for container snmp
syslog.log:44559 2026-03-10T21:18:51.034053 resolv-config: [update-containers:main] Updating resolv.conf for container what-just-happened
```

These start after 21:18:02 (when `resolv-config.service` finished and the symlink exists).
`cat /etc/resolv.conf` now follows the symlink to `/run/resolvconf/resolv.conf` and gets DNS
content. This is just per-container `postStartAction`, not the bulk update — that never fired.

So IIRC, the original intention of this change was to prevent the build server's DNS getting injected into the image. However, it looks like my initial attempt just removed the symlink itself, but didn't remove the actual file with the build server's DNS. Despite that, since the file in question is under `/run`, it would get hidden by a tmpfs mount.

This change is re-adding the symlink that was there before the `rm -f $FILESYSTEM_ROOT/etc/resolv.conf`. Given that, it might be better to just remove the `rm -f` and `touch` lines. The symlink will be there anyways.

Hi @saiarcot895, I came back to this issue and resolved the dependency issue based on your input about adding supervisor as a dependency. I was able to replicate the issue I saw above by installing supervisor with pip, and it did fail with the version mismatch when adding --constraint. I also modified line 36 in `sonic-buildimage/files/build/versions/build/build-sonic-slave-bookworm/versions-py3` to use `supervisor==4.2.5` instead of `supervisor==4.3.0`.

I also added `$(SUPERVISOR)` to `$(SONIC_SUPERVISORD_UTILITIES)_DEPENDS` in `rules/sonic-supervisord-utilities.mk` so the locally-built supervisor wheel is always available before sonic-supervisord-utilities builds, preventing pip from pulling from PyPI and corrupting the version constraint file again.

I've been running FRR 10.5.1 on our Octans EVPN Multi-Homing testbed (6-node CLOS with VPP-based SONiC) for the past few weeks and can confirm it's stable. A few observations from our parallel rebase effort that might be useful:

**1. Patch 0009 (graceful restart log levels) — missing `bgp_establish` hunk**

In 10.5.1, the GR stale timer logic that was in `bgp_establish()` was refactored into `bgp_peer_process_gr_cap_clear_stale()`. Your patch covers that correctly — but the old patch also had a hunk in `bgp_establish` (around line 2282 in 10.4.1) that changed `zlog_debug → zlog_info` for the stalepath/restart timer-stopped messages. In 10.5.1, `bgp_establish` still has a GR timer stop at ~line 2565 (`t_gr_restart` cancel + stalepath timer cancel). Can you verify those log sites in `bgp_establish` are also covered? They may have been split across the two functions.

**2. Patch 0009 — `peer->bgp->stalepath_time` vs `bgp->stalepath_time`**

Minor: in 10.5.1's `bgp_stop()`, the local variable `bgp` was introduced (`struct bgp *bgp = peer->bgp`), so the stalepath_time reference should use `bgp->stalepath_time` (not `peer->bgp->stalepath_time`). Your patch has this correct — just flagging it since it's a subtle context change from 10.4.1 that's easy to miss.

**3. Deleted patch files still in tree**

The series file correctly removes 0060–0101, but are the `.patch` files themselves deleted from `src/sonic-frr/patch/`? The diff shows them being deleted (good), but worth confirming since `stg import -s series` only reads what's in `series` — stale patch files in the directory won't break the build but will add noise to the tree.

**4. `.gitmodules` — no `branch` for FRR submodule**

The `branch = frr-10.4.1` line was removed without replacement. This is fine since the Makefile now does `git fetch origin tag $(FRR_TAG)` directly, but `git submodule update --remote` won't work without a branch hint. Not a problem for the build, but something to note for maintainers who use submodule workflows.

**5. CI failures are infra/flaky (not FRR-related)**

- `kvmtest-t0-sonic`: testbed prep failure ("Prune dangling image" on Australia East VMSS) — zero tests ran
- `kvmtest-t1-lag-vpp` (OPTIONAL): `ip/test_ip_packet.py` — 2 failures after retries, known flaky per PR comments

Overall this looks solid. The Makefile rework to use tag-based checkout (`build-$(FRR_TAG)` branch) is cleaner than the old branch-tracking approach. 👍

## Review

Clean, minimal change — adds SHA256 integrity verification for the thrift 0.14.1 tarball from `archive.apache.org`. LGTM.

**1. Suggestion: add a comment tying SHA256 to the version**

`THRIFT_VERSION` and `THRIFT_SHA256` are close together in this file so it's less of an issue than #25710, but a comment helps future maintainers:

```makefile
THRIFT_VERSION = 0.14.1
# SHA256 of thrift-THRIFT_VERSION.tar.gz - update when changing THRIFT_VERSION
THRIFT_SHA256 = 13da5e1cd9c8a3bb89778c0337cc57eb0c29b08f3090b41cf6ab78594b410ca5
```

**2. Minor: stale tarball cleanup** — the `rm -rf` at the top only removes the extracted directory (`thrift-THRIFT_VERSION`), not the tarball. The `wget -O` will overwrite it on re-run so this is fine, but for consistency you could add `rm -rf ./thrift_THRIFT_VERSION.tar.gz` before the wget (same pattern as the ifupdown2 Makefile).

Overall LGTM — approve.

## Review

Good security improvement — removes `--no-check-certificate` and adds SHA256 integrity verification. A few suggestions:

**1. Agree with @yejianquan: move `IFUPDOWN2_SHA256` to `rules/ifupdown2.mk`**

`IFUPDOWN2_VERSION` is defined there, and the SHA256 is tightly coupled to it. When someone bumps the version, they'll miss updating the hash if it lives in a different file. Suggest:

`makefile
# rules/ifupdown2.mk
IFUPDOWN2_VERSION = 3.0.0-1
IFUPDOWN2_SHA256 = 55ded1c60f720c81a5d4b5feeb48d893fd3f29d4ad0fe7d9cec508fe84b5f1b8
export IFUPDOWN2_VERSION IFUPDOWN2_SHA256
`

**2. The `-O` flag addition is correct** — without it, wget follows GitHub's CDN redirect and may save with an unexpected filename, causing the `sha256sum` check to fail.

**3. Minor: consider cleanup on hash mismatch** — if `sha256sum -c` fails, the corrupted tarball remains on disk. The `rm -rf` at the top of the recipe handles re-runs, so this is not a blocker, but worth noting.

Overall LGTM with the suggestion to move the hash constant.

> @Gfrom2016 LGTM. Clean SAI version bump with thorough verification on TH5 and TD3. Kernel module change for 5.15+ do_siocdevprivate\ looks correct.
> 
> One question: The #ifndef SIOCDEVPRIVATE\ fallback define (line 103) appears **after** its first usage on line 100 (#define NGKNET_IOC_SVTAG_SET (SIOCDEVPRIVATE + 0)). Can you confirm this compiles cleanly on kernel 5.15+ where \SIOCDEVPRIVATE\ is removed from userspace headers? If it relies on the kernel header still providing it internally, a brief comment would help.

hi @StormLiangMS , thanks for the review.
Line 100 is a macro definition, not a macro expansion:

 #define NGKNET_IOC_SVTAG_SET (SIOCDEVPRIVATE + 0)

 The C preprocessor stores (SIOCDEVPRIVATE + 0) as a literal token sequence at this point — it does not resolve SIOCDEVPRIVATE here. SIOCDEVPRIVATE is only substituted when NGKNET_IOC_SVTAG_SET is actually used in code later, by which time the #ifndef fallback on line 103 has already been processed. This is standard C preprocessor behavior.

 Additionally, since this is a kernel module (not a userspace program), it includes <linux/sockios.h> (kernel-internal), not <uapi/linux/sockios.h>. SIOCDEVPRIVATE (0x89F0) was removed from the UAPI headers in kernel 5.15+ but remains available in the kernel-internal headers — so the #ifndef guard is actually redundant here, though harmless as extra safety.

 That said, your suggestion to move the #ifndef block above the #define NGKNET_IOC_SVTAG_SET line is still a readability improvement — it makes the intent clearer to readers unfamiliar with preprocessor expansion rules. However, at the same time, I want this code to be consistent with the code provided by Broadcom.

@rustiqly @lihuay 

I agree with using flock to serialize dpkg operations instead of the current atomic mkdir based implementation. However I have some concerns:

1. The uninstallation of deb packages is still based on mkdir based serialization and it could do dpkg uninstallation parallel to the flock based installation. 

check rules/function: https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/blob/5425a55cf98f08b3ca3060341fa04e741dd70e7e/rules/functions#L157C1-L165C6

```
define UNINSTALL_DEBS
if [ -n "$(1)" ]; then \
 while true; do \
 if mkdir $(DEBS_PATH)/dpkg_lock &> /dev/null; then \
		 { sudo DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive dpkg -P $(foreach deb,$(1),$(firstword $(subst _, ,$(basename $(deb))))) $(LOG) && rm -d $(DEBS_PATH)/dpkg_lock && break; } || { rm -d $(DEBS_PATH)/dpkg_lock && exit 1 ; } \
 fi; \
 done; \
fi
endef
``` 
We should move this to flock as well.

2. Mellanox mft compilation also uses mkdir that needs to be moved to flock as well or else we could see intermittent failures during parallel builds.

check mft/Makefile: https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/blob/5425a55cf98f08b3ca3060341fa04e741dd70e7e/platform/mellanox/mft/Makefile#L80

```
	while true; do
	if mkdir $(DEST)/dpkg_lock &> /dev/null; then
	{ sudo dpkg -i $(SRC_DEB) && rm -d $(DEST)/dpkg_lock && break; } || { rm -d $(DEST)/dpkg_lock && exit 1 ; }
	fi
	done
```

CC: As this affects mellanox platform @dgsudharsan

## Copilot Review — All 22 Threads Addressed

Pushed commit `4733d4e` with fixes for all valid issues and resolved all threads.

### Code Fixes (17):
1. **`free -g` rounding** → `free -m` with `/1024` division for sub-GB precision
2. **Missing `=` regex** → added plain `=` to Makefile dep patterns
3. **CPU % cumulative** → delta between two `/proc/stat` reads 1s apart
4. **"Critical path estimate" comment** → updated to "Build parallelism analysis"
5. **60s vs 10s mismatch** → fixed section comment to say "60-second samples"
6. **Fan stats missing after-only pkgs** → `compute_fan_stats` now includes `after` relationships
7. **Missing `?=` regex** → added `?` to all regex character classes
8. **Disk I/O div-by-zero** → guard on `INTERVAL > 0`
9. **`elapsed_line` unused** → removed
10. **Redundant `LIBSWSSCOMMON_DBG`** → removed (caught by `_DBG` suffix)
11. **`active_make_jobs` in header** → removed from comment
12. **`_RDEPENDS` not used** → now populates `rdeps` dict
13. **`if v` filter on rdeps** → removed unnecessary filter
14. **`/proc/stat` idle field** → new delta code handles all 7 fields explicitly
15. **`REPORT_FORMAT` unused** → removed parameter
16. **Critical path cycles** → added `in_progress` set for cycle detection
17. **Execute permission check** → added `chmod +x` loop for companion scripts

### Not Real Issues (5) — Resolved with explanation:
18. `date -d` safety — build logs are trusted, not user input
19. CSV commas — SONiC packages follow Debian naming, no commas
20. Path traversal — build system controls `target/` paths
21. DOT red/white — standard graphviz convention, renders fine
22. `if v` redundant — harmless defensive filter (removed anyway in #13)

@prajjwal-arista 

## Code Review

The fix correctly addresses the root cause: `dhcp_dos_logger` runs `tc` commands on ports that haven't been fully re-initialized after config reload. Waiting for `PortInitDone` from `APPL_DB:PORT_TABLE` before entering the `handler()` loop is the right approach, and is already a well-established pattern in SONiC (e.g., `ports_xcvrd_notify.py`).

---

### :red_circle: Issue: No Maximum Wait Timeout — Risk of Silent Indefinite Block

`wait_for_port_init_done()` can block forever. If `PortInitDone` never arrives (e.g., `portmgrd`/`orchagent` crash, system is in a degraded state), `dhcp_dos_logger` will be silently stuck before ever entering `handler()`, providing zero monitoring. There's no fallback, no max-wait timeout, and no escalation.

Consider adding a max timeout with a warning to allow the daemon to proceed anyway:
`python
MAX_WAIT_SECONDS = 300
waited = 0
while True:
 (state, _) = sel.select(1000)
 waited += 1
 if waited >= MAX_WAIT_SECONDS:
 logger.log_warning("Timed out waiting for PortInitDone, proceeding anyway")
 return
 ...
`

---

### :yellow_circle: Minor: Inner Drain Loop Could Miss `PortInitDone` on Error Path

In the inner `while True` loop, if `sst.pop()` raises an exception (e.g., Redis connection loss), it's not caught and will propagate uncaught, crashing the daemon. The outer `handler()` loop has a `try/except`, but `wait_for_port_init_done()` does not. Worth adding:
`python
try:
 (key, op, fvp) = sst.pop()
except Exception as e:
 logger.log_error(f"Error reading from SubscriberStateTable: {e}")
 break
`

---

### :yellow_circle: Minor: Unused Variables `op` and `fvp`

In the inner loop:
`python
(key, op, fvp) = sst.pop()
`
`op` and `fvp` are never used. Use `_` to signal intent:
`python
(key, _, _) = sst.pop()
`

---

### :white_check_mark: What Looks Good

- **Core logic is correct** and mirrors the established SONiC pattern in `ports_xcvrd_notify.py` exactly. The `SubscriberStateTable` approach correctly handles both cases: waiting for a future `PortInitDone` (during reload) and detecting an already-present `PortInitDone` entry (on service restart), which explains the ~0.1s wait observed in testing.
- **Inner drain loop** (`while True: sst.pop()`) is actually an improvement over the reference — it drains multiple queued events per outer select iteration, ensuring `PortInitDone` isn't missed if other PORT_TABLE entries arrive first.
- **Import refactoring** from `swsscommon.swsscommon.ConfigDBConnector` → `swsscommon.ConfigDBConnector()` is clean and consistent.
- **Testing evidence** is well-documented in the PR description with actual log timestamps.

---

**Overall**: The approach is solid and the core logic is correct. The main concern is the **lack of a timeout guard** in `wait_for_port_init_done()` that could leave the daemon silently blocked in a degraded system state.

Thanks for the thorough review @yxieca! All addressed in the latest push:

1. **Grep injection with `+` in package names** — Replaced `grep "^${para}=="` with `awk -F'==' -v pkg="$para" '$1==pkg {print $2; exit}'`. This does exact string matching on the package name field, so `g++`, `libstdc++6`, etc. are handled correctly.

2. **`set -- $versioned_args` loses quoting** — Now uses `read -r -a` to split into a proper bash array, then `set -- "${versioned_args[@]}"` to preserve argument boundaries.

3. **Options after `install` keyword** — Added a comment explaining the behavior (option values like `bullseye` pass through safely since they won't match in versions-deb).

Also fixed Copilot's catch: `VERSION_FILE` is now `local` to avoid mutating the global.

Addressed all Copilot review comments in latest incremental commit:

1. **`VERSION_FILE` not local** — Now `local` in both `pin_apt_versions()` and `check_apt_version()`
2. **grep regex injection** — Replaced with `awk -F'==' -v pkg=...` exact string match in both functions
3. **`check_apt_version` uses `=` vs `==`** — Fixed to use awk with `==` delimiter matching versions-deb format

Hi @rustiqly, I ran a test build with the build option `SONIC_VERSION_CONTROL_COMPONENTS=py2,py3,web,git,docker,deb`. With your changes, the packages versions can be correctly pinned to those specified in the 'versions-deb' file. But this introduced a new issue. 
The versions in versions-deb files for docker/host images may contain `+fips` e.g. `openssh-server==1:10.0p1-7+fips` (this is because the images were built with build option `ENABLE_FIPS=y`). So when building these docker/host images, the version for `openssh-server` is pinned to `1:10.0p1-7+fips`, this causes `apt-get install` fail with the error message:

```

Good catch @yijingyan2! You're right — when versions-deb is generated from a FIPS build, packages like `openssh-server` get pinned to versions with a `+fips` suffix that don't exist in non-FIPS repos.

I just pushed a fix: `pin_apt_versions()` now strips the `+fips` suffix from the version string when `ENABLE_FIPS` is not `y`. This way:
- FIPS builds: version used as-is (e.g. `1:10.0p1-7+fips`)
- Non-FIPS builds: suffix stripped (e.g. `1:10.0p1-7`)

Regarding snapshot vs versions-deb conflict — they serve different purposes: snapshot pins the repo state (which packages are *available*), while versions-deb pins the *installed* versions. They should be complementary, not conflicting. But if you see issues there, happy to discuss further.

@saiarcot895 do you think this PR can be used to fix the issue related to the slowness?

No. From the man page:

```
HUP
 This lets rsyslogd perform close all open files.
```

SIGHUP does not reload the config.

@rustiqly rsyslogd v8.2504.0 doesn't support reload and HUP behavior isn't implemented to reload the config. 

We don't want to remove the sandboxing features and also we don't want the 4 second delay on every restart/warm-restart/fast-restart because rsyslog configuration doesn't change on every reboot. I suggest an alternate way:

We can add a check in rsyslog-config.sh script to restart the rsyslog service only when there is a change in the rsyslog.conf file that was currently loaded into rsyslog service. We accept the delay in the cases where we actually change the configuration (or on first boot). Changing rsyslog configuration often is not a frequent use case AFAIK. This approach should fix all the sonic-mgmt errors we currently see on various restart/warm-restart tests. Something like this:

```
sonic-cfggen -d -t /usr/share/sonic/templates/rsyslog.conf.j2 \
 -a "{\"udp_server_ip\": \"$udp_server_ip\", \"hostname\": \"$hostname\", \"docker0_ip\": \"$docker0_ip\", \"forward_with_osversion\": \"$syslog_with_osversion\", \"os_version\": \"$os_version\", \"syslog_counter\": \"$syslog_counter\"}" \
 > /tmp/rsyslog.conf.new

if ! cmp -s /tmp/rsyslog.conf.new /etc/rsyslog.conf; then
 cp /tmp/rsyslog.conf.new /etc/rsyslog.conf
 systemctl restart rsyslog
fi
rm -f /tmp/rsyslog.conf.new
```

@saiarcot895 do you agree?

Thanks @tirupatihemanth and @saiarcot895 — you're absolutely right that SIGHUP doesn't reload rsyslog config. Reworked the approach completely:

**Before:** Attempted `reload-or-restart` with SIGHUP (doesn't work)
**After:** Conditional restart — generate config to temp file, compare with current, only restart when config changed.

```bash
sonic-cfggen ... > /tmp/rsyslog.conf.new
if ! cmp -s /tmp/rsyslog.conf.new /etc/rsyslog.conf; then
 cp /tmp/rsyslog.conf.new /etc/rsyslog.conf
 systemctl restart rsyslog
fi
rm -f /tmp/rsyslog.conf.new
```

This avoids the ~4s Trixie sandboxing delay on reboot/warm-restart/fast-restart where rsyslog config is unchanged. Removed the systemd ExecReload override entirely.

Updated PR title and description to match.

**Local logic verification:**

```
=== Test: config unchanged → no restart ===
SKIP (config unchanged) ✅

=== Test: config changed → restart === 
RESTART (config changed) ✅

=== Test: no existing config (first boot) → restart ===
RESTART (first boot, no existing config) ✅
```

Three cases tested:
1. **Config unchanged** (reboot/warm-restart) → skip restart, avoid 4s Trixie delay ✅
2. **Config changed** (syslog server modified) → restart as before ✅
3. **First boot** (no existing config) → restart to apply initial config ✅

### Copilot Review Thread Resolution

All 5 Copilot review threads have been addressed in the current code:

1. **PR description mismatch** — The PR title mentions "reload" but the implementation evolved to a smarter approach: skip the restart entirely when config is unchanged. This avoids the 4s Trixie delay without needing a reload mechanism. Updated PR description would be nice but the code is correct.

2. **/tmp vs PrivateTmp** — Not an issue. This script runs as a host process (invoked by rsyslog-config.service), not inside rsyslog's namespace. PrivateTmp only affects rsyslog's own view of /tmp, not the host script that generates its config.

3. **Temp file cleanup** — Already handled: `trap 'rm -f "$TMPFILE"' EXIT` + `mktemp` pattern.

4. **cp error handling** — Already handled: `cp ... || { echo ... >&2; exit 1; }`.

5. **cmp fails if /etc/rsyslog.conf missing** — Already handled: `cmp -s ... 2>/dev/null` suppresses the error, and the condition correctly falls through to copy+restart on first boot.

Resolving all threads.

## CI Failure Analysis: `syslog/test_logrotate.py` on t0 and multi-asic-t1

### Root Cause

`test_logrotate.py` mounts a small loopback device over `/var/log`, then calls `config_reload()`. During config_reload, `rsyslog-config.sh` runs:

- **Before this PR**: Always runs `systemctl restart rsyslog` → rsyslog re-opens `/var/log/syslog` on the new mount ✅
- **After this PR**: Skips restart if config unchanged → rsyslog keeps writing to the old file descriptor (hidden under the new mount) → test sees empty/stale `/var/log/syslog` ❌

The `cmp` optimization is correct for the config-only case, but breaks when the filesystem has changed underneath rsyslog.

### Suggested Fix

Instead of skipping rsyslog entirely when config is unchanged, use `systemctl reload rsyslog` (sends SIGHUP):

```bash
if ! cmp -s "$TMPFILE" /etc/rsyslog.conf 2>/dev/null; then
 cp "$TMPFILE" /etc/rsyslog.conf || { echo "Failed to update /etc/rsyslog.conf" >&2; exit 1; }
 systemctl restart rsyslog
else
 # Config unchanged, but rsyslog may need to re-open log files
 # (e.g., after /var/log remount). Reload is fast (~0ms vs ~4s restart on Trixie).
 systemctl reload rsyslog
fi
```

This preserves the Trixie performance fix (no 4s restart delay) while ensuring rsyslog always re-reads its config and re-opens file handles.

### Other Failures

The other 4 failed jobs (t1-lag, dualtor, t2, vpp) are all `LOCK_TESTBED_FAILED` — infra/resource contention, unrelated to this PR.

Fix pushed in c3ae8cc5c: replaced the skip-if-unchanged approach with `reload-or-restart` + an `ExecReload` systemd drop-in.

**Root cause of test_logrotate.py failure:** The previous approach skipped rsyslog restart when the config file was unchanged. But `test_logrotate.py` remounts `/var/log` to a different device — rsyslog still has file descriptors pointing to the old filesystem. It needs a restart/reload regardless of config changes.

**Fix:**
- Always write config and call `systemctl reload-or-restart rsyslog`
- Add `/etc/systemd/system/rsyslog.service.d/reload.conf` with `ExecReload=/bin/kill -HUP $MAINPID` so rsyslog can do in-place config reload via SIGHUP
- On systems without the drop-in, `reload-or-restart` falls back to `restart` (same as before)
- On Trixie with the drop-in, rsyslog reloads in-place with zero downtime

### Root cause analysis of CI failures

All 3 KVM test topologies (t0, t1-lag, t2) failed on **`syslog/test_logrotate.py`** (2 test functions each).

**Root cause:** The previous conditional-restart approach (`cmp -s` + skip) broke tests that call `config_reload` after filesystem changes:

1. `test_logrotate_small_size` mounts a new filesystem on `/var/log`
2. Calls `config_reload` → triggers `rsyslog-config.sh`
3. Old code: skip restart because config unchanged
4. rsyslog keeps stale file descriptors to the old `/var/log` mount
5. New `/var/log/syslog` never gets written → logrotate assertions fail

Same issue affects `test_logrotate_full_partition_no_archives_cleanup`.

**Fix:** Completely reworked the approach. Instead of skipping the restart (working around the symptom), fix the root cause:

- **Trixie's rsyslog.service** has `PrivateTmp=yes`, `ProtectSystem=full`, `ProtectKernelTunables=yes`, etc.
- systemd sets up and tears down mount/PID/network namespaces on every start/stop — that's the ~4s overhead
- Added a **systemd drop-in override** that resets all sandboxing directives to defaults (disabled)
- rsyslog-config.sh is **reverted to original behavior** (always restart)

This is the correct fix because SONiC runs rsyslog as root on the host — the sandboxing adds no practical security value but costs 4 seconds per restart.

@tirupatihemanth @saiarcot895 @yxieca — the drop-in approach addresses the same issue as the conditional restart but without the test breakage. PTAL.

Reworked to restore the conditional-restart implementation as requested. The key difference from the earlier version (which broke `test_logrotate`): when config is unchanged, we now send **SIGHUP** instead of doing nothing. This makes rsyslog re-open its log files, which fixes the logrotate test failure (rsyslog needs to pick up new file handles after `/var/log` remounts).

- Config **unchanged** → `systemctl kill -s HUP rsyslog` (instant, re-opens files, no namespace teardown)

**Sandboxing preserved** — no systemd drop-in override, no security posture change.

- `[ ! -f /etc/rsyslog.conf ]` guard for first boot
- `cp` failure handling (don't restart with stale config)

## t0 Test Failure Analysis

The t0 KVM test failure (build 1076116) is **unrelated to this PR** (rsyslog config change). Two flaky tests caused the failure:

### 1. `dhcp_relay/test_dhcpv4_relay.py` — Race condition (2 failures, triggered stop-on-failure)

The PTF test has a race between sniffer thread startup and packet sending — no synchronization barrier between `Thread.start()` and `client_send_discover()`. On loaded KVM workers, the scapy socket is not ready when the packet fires.

**Proof:** The same test also failed on completely unrelated PR #26545 (build 1078496) with identical error pattern. Recent merged PRs (#26535, #26528, #26527, #26534) all passed t0 clean.

Filed: sonic-net/sonic-mgmt#23636

### 2. `hash/test_generic_hash.py` — Module timeout at 20.55 min

The `test_reboot` function does a full device reboot + two PTF traffic runs. Average runtime is ~16 min, dangerously close to the 20-min module timeout. Slow KVM disk I/O on loaded workers pushes it over.

Filed: sonic-net/sonic-mgmt#23637

### Our change is clean
All 4 syslog-related tests (`test_logrotate`, `test_syslog`, `test_syslog_rate_limit`, `test_syslog_source_ip`) were included in the t0 run and **passed**. The 878 passing tests include our impacted area.

@saiarcot895 Great question. `do_config_initialization()` is called from `boot_config()` in two code paths:

1. When `/tmp/pending_config_initialization` or the `CONFIG_SETUP_INITIALIZATION_FLAG` exists (first-time boot after install)
2. When `config_db.json` does not exist at all (e.g., upgrade path tests that deliberately delete it)

In both cases, without this minigraph check, the function falls through to the ZTP/factory-default logic: ZTP generates a minimal config via `ztp-profile.sh` and then runs `config reload`, which wipes the management IP configured by minigraph — requiring console recovery.

This IS the right place for the check because `do_config_initialization()` is the decision point for "config_db.json is missing, what do we do?". The priority order should be:
- `config_db.json` exists -> handled before we even enter this function
- `minigraph.xml` exists -> use `reload_minigraph` (this new check)
- ZTP enabled -> generate ZTP config (existing behavior)
- Otherwise -> generate factory default (existing behavior)

Note that `do_config_migration()` (the image-upgrade path) already has this exact pattern — it checks for minigraph.xml as a fallback when config_db.json is missing (line 404). This change aligns `do_config_initialization()` with the same logic.

On images where ZTP is disabled, and a `minigraph.xml` file is already present, the `minigraph.xml` file is used, and a factory-default configuration is not generated. If that was already working without ZTP, then that suggests that this new `reload_minigraph` check is not required for the flow you described.

@saiarcot895 Good point — let me clarify.

When ZTP is **disabled** and `config_db.json` is missing, the existing code takes the factory default path (line 289-293):
```
generate_config factory CONFIG_DB_JSON
reload_configdb CONFIG_DB_JSON
```
This generates a factory default `config_db.json` and loads it via `config reload`. It does **not** use minigraph.xml — the device gets a factory default config, losing whatever was configured via minigraph. So even without ZTP, the device ends up with a factory default config rather than its minigraph-based config.

However, you're right that the **critical** bug is with ZTP **enabled** — that's the scenario from ADO 36697420. When ZTP is enabled, `ztp-profile.sh` creates a minimal ZTP config and `config reload` wipes the management IP, making the device unreachable.

The minigraph check addresses both cases:
1. **ZTP enabled** (the reported bug): Prevents ZTP from generating a config and running `config reload` that wipes mgmt IP
2. **ZTP disabled**: Ensures the device uses its minigraph config instead of falling back to a factory default

Both are consistent with the pattern in `do_config_migration()` (line 404) which already prefers minigraph over factory default when `config_db.json` is missing during image upgrades.

Would you prefer we scope this fix to only the ZTP-enabled case (e.g., guard just the ZTP block at line 295)?

### Suggested Test Plan

**3 code changes, 4 test scenarios:**

#### Scenario A — Primary bug fix (ZTP enabled + minigraph present)
1. Build image with this change, ZTP enabled
2. Place `minigraph.xml` on device, delete `config_db.json`
3. Cold boot
4. **Expected:** Device uses `reload_minigraph`, management IP preserved, no ZTP config reload

#### Scenario B — Regression check (ZTP enabled, no minigraph)
1. ZTP enabled, no `config_db.json`, no `minigraph.xml`
2. Cold boot
3. **Expected:** ZTP triggers as before (existing behavior preserved)

#### Scenario C — Factory default regression (ZTP disabled + minigraph present)
1. ZTP disabled, `minigraph.xml` present, no `config_db.json`
2. Cold boot
3. **Expected:** Device uses `reload_minigraph` instead of factory default

#### Scenario D — Warm boot guard
1. ZTP enabled, `minigraph.xml` present, no `config_db.json`
2. Warm boot (`SONIC_BOOT_TYPE=warm` in `/proc/cmdline`)
3. **Expected:** Config initialization and ZTP skipped entirely, existing running config preserved

#### Verification commands:
- `show runningconfiguration all` — verify config matches minigraph expectations
- `show ip interface` — verify management IP is intact
- `ztp status` — verify ZTP was not triggered (Scenarios A, C, D)
- `grep "minigraph" /var/log/syslog` — confirm `reload_minigraph` was called (Scenarios A, C)
- `grep "Warm boot detected" /var/log/syslog` — confirm warm boot guard (Scenario D)

#### Upgrade path test (end-to-end):
Run the existing upgrade path test suite (which deletes `config_db.json`) on a ZTP-enabled 202511 image. The device should remain reachable via management IP throughout the test — this was the original failure mode reported in ADO 36697420.

### Complete `config-setup boot` workflow after this change

```
boot_config() called
│
├─ 1. check_system_warm_boot()
│ ├─ Check /proc/cmdline for SONIC_BOOT_TYPE=warm ← NEW
│ │ └─ If found → WARM_BOOT="true", return
│ └─ Fall back: check STATE_DB WARM_RESTART_ENABLE_TABLE
│ └─ If enable=true → WARM_BOOT="true"
│
├─ 2. Pending config migration?
│ (/tmp/pending_config_migration or CONFIG_SETUP_POST_MIGRATION_FLAG)
│ └─ Yes → do_config_migration()
│ ├─ Copy files from old_config (minigraph, config_db, frr, etc.)
│ ├─ Run post-migration hooks
│ ├─ WARM_BOOT=true? → do_db_migration, exit 0 (script ends)
│ ├─ config_db.json present? → reload_configdb, do_db_migration
│ ├─ minigraph.xml present? → reload_minigraph, do_db_migration
│ └─ Neither → log warning
│
├─ 3. WARM_BOOT=true? → "Warm boot detected, skipping", return ← NEW
│ (No config initialization or ZTP during warm boot)
│
├─ 4. Multi-ASIC (NUM_ASIC > 1)? → return
│ (Config initialization not supported for multi-NPU)
│
├─ 5. Pending config initialization?
│ (/tmp/pending_config_initialization or CONFIG_SETUP_INITIALIZATION_FLAG)
│ └─ Yes → do_config_initialization()
│
├─ 6. config_db.json missing?
│ └─ Yes → do_config_initialization()
│ │
│ │ do_config_initialization():
│ │ ├─ minigraph.xml present? ← NEW
│ │ │ └─ Yes → reload_minigraph, set CONFIG_DB_INITIALIZED, return
│ │ ├─ ZTP disabled?
│ │ │ └─ generate factory default, reload_configdb
│ │ └─ ZTP enabled?
│ │ └─ generate ZTP config, reload_configdb
│ │
│ └─ After do_config_initialization():
│ └─ minigraph.xml absent AND ZTP enabled? ← UPDATED guard
│ ├─ ZTP SUCCESS/FAILED → ztp erase -y
│ └─ Otherwise → touch /tmp/pending_ztp_restart
│
└─ Done
```

**Key changes (marked with ← NEW / ← UPDATED):**

1. **`check_system_warm_boot()`** — now checks `/proc/cmdline` for `SONIC_BOOT_TYPE=warm` first, consistent with `getBootType()` used in `docker_image_ctl.j2`, `syncd_common.sh`, and `watchdog-control.sh`. Falls back to STATE_DB for compatibility.
2. **Step 3 warm boot guard** — during warm boot, skip all config initialization and ZTP entirely. Existing running config must be preserved.
3. **`do_config_initialization()` minigraph check** — if `minigraph.xml` is present, use `reload_minigraph` and return early. Aligns with the pattern already in `do_config_migration()`.
4. **ZTP restart block** — guarded with `[ ! -e minigraph.xml ]` so ZTP erase/restart is not triggered when minigraph was used.

**Config priority order (consistent across all paths):**
`config_db.json` → `minigraph.xml` → ZTP → factory default

@saiarcot895 Thanks for pushing on this — I want to clarify an important distinction between two code paths.

You're right that when `minigraph.xml` is present, it repo_list.sh used during **image upgrades**. That's because the upgrade path goes through `do_config_migration()` (triggered by `pending_config_migration`), which already checks for minigraph as a fallback at line 404:

```bash
elif [ -r ${MINGRAPH_FILE} ]; then
 echo "Use minigraph.xml from old system..."
 reload_minigraph
```

So in the normal upgrade scenario, minigraph works correctly — that path was already fine.

However, `do_config_initialization()` is a **different code path** that is called when:
1. A `pending_config_initialization` flag exists (first-time boot after install)
2. `config_db.json` simply doesn't exist and there's no pending migration

In the **original** `do_config_initialization()`, there is no minigraph check at all:

```bash
# Original code
do_config_initialization()
{
 if ! ztp_is_enabled ; then
 generate_config factory ${CONFIG_DB_JSON}
 reload_configdb ${CONFIG_DB_JSON} # factory default, ignores minigraph
 fi
 if ztp_is_enabled ; then
 generate_config ztp ... # ZTP config, ignores minigraph
 reload_configdb ...
 fi
}
```

The reported bug (ADO 36697420) hits this initialization path specifically: the upgrade path tests delete `config_db.json` **without** setting the `pending_config_migration` flag, so `do_config_migration()` is never called. Instead, `boot_config()` sees no `config_db.json` and calls `do_config_initialization()`, which falls through to ZTP (or factory default), ignoring the minigraph.

So to summarize:
- **`do_config_migration()`** (upgrade path) — already handles minigraph ✅
- **`do_config_initialization()`** (no migration, no config_db) — did NOT handle minigraph ❌ ← this PR fixes it

The fix aligns `do_config_initialization()` with the same priority order that `do_config_migration()` already uses.

Reproduced this issue several times in a DooD build environment (SONIC_CONFIG_USE_NATIVE_DOCKERD_FOR_BUILD=y), for platform=broadcom, arch=amd64

It looks like test_minigraph_packet_chassis_acl_local_host is flawed. Shouldn't the fix be made there instead?

Thanks for reproducing this @sertem-c! You raise a valid point about the test itself.

The root cause is that `get_path_to_platform_dir()` returns `None` when running outside a platform context (e.g., DooD builds, VS builds at certain parallelism levels). Our fix guards the callers (`asic_sensors_config.py` and `smartswitch_config.py`) so they return empty dicts gracefully instead of crashing.

The test `test_minigraph_packet_chassis_acl_local_host` exercises a code path that calls these functions, and it's the test that surfaces the crash — but the bug is in the production code not handling the None return, not in the test setup. Fixing only the test would leave the crash in production for any real scenario where platform_dir is unavailable.

That said, the test could also be improved to mock the platform path — happy to add that as a follow-up if reviewers prefer.

## Test Results — SONIC_BUILD_MEMORY_FLAGS

Tested both Makefile logic expansion and Docker runtime behavior.

### Makefile Logic Tests (6/6 pass)

Using a test harness that copies the exact `SONIC_BUILD_MEMORY_FLAGS` logic from `Makefile.work`:

| # | Scenario | Expected | Result |
|---|----------|----------|--------|
| 1 | `SONIC_BUILD_MEMORY` unset (default) | No `--memory` flag | ✅ PASS |
| 2 | `SONIC_BUILD_MEMORY=24g` | `--memory=24g` | ✅ PASS |
| 3 | `SONIC_BUILD_MEMORY=24g` + `SONIC_BUILD_MEMORY_SWAP=48g` | Both flags | ✅ PASS |
| 4 | `SONIC_BUILD_MEMORY=none` (explicit disable) | No `--memory` flag | ✅ PASS |
| 5 | Memory flag placement | Before `-e http_proxy` | ✅ PASS |
| 6 | `SONIC_BUILDER_EXTRA_CMDLINE` stays last | `--cpus=4` at end | ✅ PASS |

### Docker Runtime Tests (4/4 pass)

| # | Scenario | Expected | Result |
|---|----------|----------|--------|
| 7 | `--memory=128m` → cgroup limit | `134217728` (128MB) | ✅ PASS |
| 8 | No `--memory` → unlimited | `max` | ✅ PASS |
| 9 | OOM contained in container | Exit 137 (SIGKILL), host unaffected | ✅ PASS |
| 10 | `--memory=128m --memory-swap=128m` | memory=128MB, swap=0 | ✅ PASS |

All 10 tests pass. The feature works as designed: opt-in memory limit, OOM kills stay inside the container, and `SONIC_BUILDER_EXTRA_CMDLINE` can still override.

## Integration Test — Build-Like Workload Under Memory Constraint

Simulated a real build scenario: compile + run a memory-hungry process inside a Docker container, with and without `--memory`.

### Test Setup
- Container image: `debian:bookworm-slim` + `build-essential`
- Workload: `make` builds and runs a C program that allocates 10MB chunks until OOM
- Host: 30GB RAM

### Results

**Test A — WITHOUT `--memory` (current behavior):**
```
Allocated 13560MB
Allocated 13570MB
...
Allocated 13630MB
```
Process consumed **13.6GB** of host RAM before being externally killed (timeout). Without the timeout, it would eat all 30GB and trigger the **kernel OOM killer**, which can kill **any** host process — including your desktop, SSH sessions, or other builds.

**Test B — WITH `--memory=64m` (this PR):**
```
Allocated 40MB
Allocated 50MB
...
Allocated 120MB
make: 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025 2026 parse_reviews.py pr_dump.sh repo_list.sh repos update.sh [Makefile:3: all] Killed
Exit code: 137 (SIGKILL from cgroup OOM)
```
Process OOM-killed at the container boundary. Host unaffected.

**Test C — Host verification after OOM:**
```
 total used free
Mem: 30Gi 7.8Gi 14Gi
```
Host RAM untouched.

### Kernel Log Confirmation
```
oom-kill:constraint=CONSTRAINT_MEMCG,...task=mem_hog,pid=76693
Memory cgroup out of memory: Killed process 76693 (mem_hog) total-vm:135648kB, anon-rss:61248kB
```
The kill was triggered by `CONSTRAINT_MEMCG` (Docker cgroup limit) — **not** the system-wide OOM killer. This is exactly the behavior the PR enables: OOM stays contained inside the build container.

Hi @lipxu what do you think about adding a `-th` suffix throughout the new "legacy" naming introduced in this PR? For example docker-syncd-brcm-legacy -> docker-syncd-brcm-legacy-th

This should reduce confusion in future when there is a second brcm platform that is EOL which would become another but different legacy version.
hi @lipxu Would you clarify why we need to change so many files like what we discussed in the description part? Did you test this change with master branch?

> Hi @lipxu what do you think about adding a `-th` suffix throughout the new "legacy" naming introduced in this PR? For example docker-syncd-brcm-legacy -> docker-syncd-brcm-legacy-th
> 
> This should reduce confusion in future when there is a second brcm platform that is EOL which would become another but different legacy version.

Thanks for your suggestion. The image was originally named legacy rather than legacy-th because it was intended for a special-case scenario and was expected to be used rarely.
We may consider renaming it to legacy-th if we think a similar solution is needed for other platforms in the future.

> hi @lipxu Would you clarify why we need to change so many files like what we discussed in the description part? Did you test this change with master branch?

Since the legacy ASIC type uses a compatible SAI binary built with a different Broadcom SDK version, we need to include the matching kernel modules and drivers. Thanks.
I haven’t tested it on the master branch yet. I only built a private image based on 202511 and ran the nightly tests.
However, since the PR has passed all the PR checks, I believe it should also work fine on the master branch.

> @lipxu Review comments:
> 
> **Blockers:**
> 
> 1. *_Hardcoded kernel version in \debian/opennsl-modules.install*_ — Install paths reference \lib/modules/6.12.41+deb13-sonic-amd64/extra. This will break on any kernel version change. Should be generated from \KVERSION\ at build time.
> 2. *_\sudo\ in \debian/rules*_ — Package build uses \sudo\ to mutate host paths under /lib/modules\ and /usr/src. This is unsafe in CI and may fail in non-root build containers. Should use \KDIR/\KERNDIR\ to pass kernel header paths instead.
> 
> **Question:**
> 
> 3. **~580 vendored source files** — The entire \saibcm-modules-legacy/\ kernel module source tree is checked into the repo. Is there a submodule or package alternative to avoid carrying this in-tree? If vendoring is the only option, please confirm in the PR description.

Thank you for your detailed review @StormLiangMS ! I'd like to clarify the scope:

### Blockers:

**1. Hardcoded kernel version in `debian/opennsl-modules.install`**
**2. `sudo` in `debian/rules`**

Both of these are **pre-existing issues** that have existed in the codebase since the original implementation and are currently present in:
- `platform/broadcom/saibcm-modules/debian/` (XGS family - in production)
- `platform/broadcom/saibcm-modules-dnx/debian/` (DNX family - in production)

**Key points:**
- These patterns were **not introduced by this PR** 
- These modules have been **building and running successfully in production for a long time** without issues reported
- This PR maintains **consistency** with the established codebase patterns

**Risk Assessment:**
Given that both existing modules work correctly with these patterns, the risk is minimal. 

**Recommendation:**
1. **Merge this PR** - It follows established patterns and adds Legacy ASIC support without introducing new technical debt
2. **Create a follow-up task** - Address these architectural concerns across all three modules (saibcm-modules, saibcm-modules-dnx, and saibcm-modules-legacy) in a unified refactoring effort

This approach ensures we don't block the Legacy ASIC feature while acknowledging the technical debt can be improved across the board.

### Question:

**~665 vendored source files**

The `saibcm-modules-legacy` source tree is currently vendored directly (not a submodule), consistent with the existing `saibcm-modules` module.

**Future plan:**
We plan to follow the `saibcm-modules-dnx` pattern and convert this to a git submodule in the future. However, this refactoring would:
- Require significant effort and a long time to set up a separate repository
- Need thorough validation across all xgs and legacy ASIC platforms

**Recommendation:**
We prefer to merge the current implementation now (matching the established `saibcm-modules` pattern) to unblock Legacy ASIC support, then improve it in a follow-up effort.

> > Hi @lipxu what do you think about adding a `-th` suffix throughout the new "legacy" naming introduced in this PR? For example docker-syncd-brcm-legacy -> docker-syncd-brcm-legacy-th
> > This should reduce confusion in future when there is a second brcm platform that is EOL which would become another but different legacy version.
>

Full PTF-tests on NOKIA boards

@Pterosaur @r12f 
This change will make countersyncd run regardless to whether or not HFT is even configured.
This will waste resources when HFT is disabled.

Also the way that it is set, countersyncd will always run with only enable-otel flag. The service countersyncd also provides flags such as otel-endpoint or otel-capacity flags - which are not configurable by this implementation.

I suggested to keep it as a service that will only start in response to a CLI command by the user or a DB configuration
Azure Pipelines successfully started running 1 pipeline(s).

> @Pterosaur @r12f This change will make countersyncd run regardless to whether or not HFT is even configured. This will waste resources when HFT is disabled.
> 
> Also the way that it is set, countersyncd will always run with only enable-otel flag. The service countersyncd also provides flags such as otel-endpoint or otel-capacity flags - which are not configurable by this implementation.
> 
> I suggested to keep it as a service that will only start in response to a CLI command by the user or a DB configuration

Hi @DavidZagury Thanks for your suggestion
1. It's impossible to connect the CLI with the countersyncd service. Because we have multiple methods to config HFT. Cli is just one of them. In the most time, we will use GNMI to config it which means the GNMI will directly inject configuration into config db instead of using CLI.
2. In my humble opinion, if there is no data telemetry traffic under the countersyncd which means the HFT feature isn't enabled or configured, the countersyncd will be almost silent. It will only use tens of megabytes of memory and almost zero CPU.
3. To the flag `otel-endpoint`, it's not a standard method to configure the endpoint of otel. The better way is to use the config db that mentioned in: https://github.com/sonic-net/SONiC/blob/master/doc/high-frequency-telemetry/high-frequency-telemetry-hld.md#742-high_frequency_telemetry_profile But we haven't supported that. This flag is exposed right now that is only used for simplifying testing flow.

> > Hi @jusherma , 202505 has been closed for long time and only take mandatory must-have fixes, I can see 202505 image build is running well. Can you let me know what's the big issues in your internal builds? Also the rule is applicable to other older feature branches you requested.
>

Hi @jusherma, for the builds to succeed without MIRROR_SNAPSHOT, you can add patches to branches.
For branch 202505, edit `rules/snmpd.mk`:
replace 
```
ifeq ($(BLDENV),bookworm)
SNMPD_VERSION = 5.9.3+dfsg
SNMPD_VERSION_FULL = $(SNMPD_VERSION)-2
```
with
```
ifeq ($(BLDENV),bookworm)
SNMPD_VERSION = 5.9.3+dfsg
SNMPD_VERSION_FULL = $(SNMPD_VERSION)-2+deb12u1
```

For branch 202411 and 202405, eidt `rules/snmpd.mk`:
replace
```
ifeq ($(BLDENV),bookworm)
SNMPD_VERSION = 5.9.3+dfsg
SNMPD_VERSION_FULL = $(SNMPD_VERSION)-2
else ifeq ($(BLDENV),bullseye)
SNMPD_VERSION = 5.9+dfsg
SNMPD_VERSION_FULL = $(SNMPD_VERSION)-4+deb11u1
```
with
```
ifeq ($(BLDENV),bookworm)
SNMPD_VERSION = 5.9.3+dfsg
SNMPD_VERSION_FULL = $(SNMPD_VERSION)-2+deb12u1
else ifeq ($(BLDENV),bullseye)
SNMPD_VERSION = 5.9+dfsg
SNMPD_VERSION_FULL = $(SNMPD_VERSION)-4+deb11u3
```

@yijingyan2 We have customers that expect to build SONiC with our platform code, using the sonic-net upstream code. We do not want to maintain patches and ask customers to apply them before building. We need the sonic-net code to build as-is, without any proprietary trafficmanager domains required. We need the libsnmp fixes actually committed. We cannot take these to customers as patches

@jusherma , When using debian mirror and Pypi packages, we can't ensure build is always good. Because dependencies changed frequently. Maybe we need to fix build issues every week. We can't keep fixing these build issues as daily work.

> @jusherma , When using debian mirror and Pypi packages, we can't ensure build is always good. Because dependencies changed frequently. Maybe we need to fix build issues every week. We can't keep fixing these build issues as daily work.

We've been successfully building without MIRROR_SNAPSHOT for a long time now. This is the first issue we've seen. Regardless, the solution to version drift cannot be that there's a dependency on a proprietary, Microsoft-internal mirror that sometimes [goes down for a week at a time](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/issues/20547)

hi @lipxu could you help to clarify what does the datastore lock for in the description? And why we could take it as a signal of mgmtd initial config done?

> hi @lipxu could you help to clarify what does the datastore lock for in the description? And why we could take it as a signal of mgmtd initial config done?

Thanks Storm, The datastore lock is used to prevent other processes from accessing the datastore while mgmtd is using it.
During startup, mgmtd acquires the datastore lock and releases it only after the initial configuration load is complete.
Therefore, if the script reports that the datastore is not locked, it is safe to assume that mgmtd has finished its initial configuration.

> > @wumiaont can you clarify benefit of adding this in show output. Does each vendor need to define fips module name in platform file.
> 
> @judyjoseph FIPS certificate will list the macsec FIPS module name on the certificate. FIPS lab required SONiC to have a CLI command to display the macsec FIPS module name so security officer in the data center can check the deployed system is running the same module as FIPS certificate shows.
> 
> Yes. This FIPS module name is vendor specific. Each vendor can provide this name in the vendor specific platform.json and this CLI will display it. If vendor doesn't provide it will have no output for this command.

Sure, so so this name "Nokia-SONiC-MACsec-Version 1.0" signify something? (like a particular version of SAI macsec library, ssl library etc )

> Nokia-SONiC-MACsec-Version 1.0

Nokia-SONiC-MACsec-Version 1.0 is the name for the FIPS module on Nokia chassis. This includes software (BCOM SDK 6.5.34 FIPS module) + hardware (BCOM J2c+ 88852 chip). This name will be put in the certificate and future security officer uses this CLI command to check chassis to make sure it runs with the exact FIPS module.

> what about `platform/vs/docker-sonic-vs/etc/rsyslog.conf` don't need this change?

Strictly speaking, it doesn't need this change, because the logs aren't actually being forwarded anywhere. It'll forward it to localhost port 514, but there likely won't be anything listening on this port. That container doesn't end up on the device.

It would be nice to update the syntax there to have it use the new syntax, but I'll keep that separate for now.

> This is kind of new feature. Is it possible to config for new behavior or old behavior?

@qiluo-msft While it could be added as a configurable feature, this would require an application (or daemon) running in each of the containers to determine which method to use (RELP or UDP). The last time such a feature was added (#12490), it had to be reworked to be disabled by default (#17458). Given that there are currently issues seen on cold bootup (#25382) and warm reboot (#17792), I'd like this to be enabled by default for simplicity.
